body_txt: Mushrooms that are larger than average appear often in fantasy. While some actual mushrooms have been known to grow as large as soccer balls, in fiction mushrooms can grow to be as large as people, or even trees, sometimes leading to whole forests of nothing but mushrooms. Visually, these are most commonly classic wide-capped toadstools, often in vivid colors; less often, other forms such as cup fungi, shelf mushrooms, or puffballs may appear in giant-sized versions. Giant mushrooms, alone or in forests, are typically associated with distant and exotic environments — you won't commonly find tree-sized fungi lining the streets of a suburb or among the maples of a backyard wood — and are a fairly common choice for unusual flora used to convey the alien nature of a place, when unreal or differently sized trees, grass, and the like just wouldn't seem alien enough. In particular, forests of giant mushrooms and mushroom-like growths are a common sight on alien planets, and are a staple replacement for photosynthetic plants in the dark depths of Fantastic Underworlds . Huge toadstools might also be found in an Enchanted Forest , scattered amid the undergrowth or reaching into the canopy. In more mundane environments, they often show up in swamplands and in jungles. Other times, they are used to show corruption, an evil blight that is spreading across the land. This can lead to Fridge Logic issues if an entire planetary ecosystem is based on this trope, as even giant fungi can't produce oxygen like plants can. This does not apply to tiny people living in regular mushrooms , since in those instances it is the people that are unusual, not the mushrooms. This also does not apply to mushroom clouds , which are just mushroom-shaped. Nor does it apply to sapient fungi, since they are not necessarily part of the scenery, unless they are so humongous that they dominate the scene. See Mushroom Man for that sort of thing. Of course, if they live in giant mushrooms, that is another matter. If it's in a video game level, the mushrooms may be bouncy . Named after the wordplay "a fungus among us". See Towering Flower for its floral equivalent.
